children.name: Ifrita
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Blue-capped Ifrita, a small, active passerine endemic to the montane forests of New Guinea. It is a remarkable bird in many ways: it is one of the few known passerines to possess a powerful neurotoxin, batrachotoxin, in its skin and feathers, and its plumage combines vivid blue, yellow, and black in a distinctive pattern. It was long placed with the whistlers.

children.name: Ifrita kowaldi
children.description: A small, active passerine with a brilliant blue crown, yellow underparts, and a bold black stripe through the eye, found in the highland forests of New Guinea from 1,500 to 3,500 metres. It is one of the few known poisonous birds, with batrachotoxin in its skin and feathers that can cause numbness or irritation in humans. It forages for insects in a nuthatch-like manner, creeping along moss-covered branches.
